original_hypothesis.title: Isolate Auction First Bid Minimum Equals Borrow Amount Creating Permanent Bad Debt
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The first bid in an isolate auction must be >= vars.borrowAmount AND >= vars.liquidatePrice. If the NFT floor price drops significantly below the accumulated debt (borrowAmount >> NFT value), no rational bidder will bid because they'd pay more than the NFT is worth. The loan becomes permanently stuck: can't be liquidated (no bidder), can't be repaid by owner (they abandoned it), creating unrecoverable bad debt. calculateNftLoanLiquidatePrice confirms the liquidatePrice is bounded by borrowAmount from below.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Unlike cross-margin liquidation where collateral is ERC20 (always has some liquidation-worthy value), NFT collateral can become worthless while debt accumulates. The auction mechanism has no fallback for worthless collateral, leading to systemic bad debt accumulation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(params.amounts[vars.nidx] >= vars.borrowAmount) enforces bid >= debt; when debt >> NFT value, no rational bidder participates

preconditions: Accumulated debt with interest exceeds NFT market value
No bidder willing to take a guaranteed loss on the auction

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: First bid requires bidAmount >= borrowAmount, but borrowAmount can exceed NFT value. liquidatePrice = max(nftPrice * (1 - bonus), borrowAmount) so liquidatePrice >= borrowAmount.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Not active manipulation; systemic risk from market conditions
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No profit; bad debt locks lender funds permanently

economic_questions: What percentage of historical NFT price drops would trigger this scenario?
Is there a governance mechanism to write off bad debt?
Can the protocol socialize the loss or force liquidation at market price?
validation_requirements: Simulate NFT price drop: verify no rational bid is possible
Check if admin can intervene to close stuck positions
Quantify potential bad debt accumulation under various market scenarios

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: N/A - market condition issue, not active attack

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Lender funds locked; cannot be withdrawn; supplier bank run risk

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All lenders of the borrowed asset
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Proportional to bad debt vs total pool liquidity
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Sum of all underwater isolate loan debt

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Historical NFT price volatility vs configured LTV/liquidation thresholds (6000-8000 bp)
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Model bad debt accumulation under various drawdown scenarios

validation_result.mechanism: auction minimum bid floor creates unliquidatable underwater positions
validation_result.impact_summary: When accumulated debt exceeds NFT collateral value, the isolate auction mechanism becomes non-functional because the first bid must be >= borrowAmount. No rational bidder will participate, creating permanent bad debt and locking lender funds.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis accurately identifies a critical design gap in the isolate auction mechanism. The code in executeIsolateAuction enforces that the first bid must be >= vars.borrowAmount AND >= vars.liquidatePrice. The calculateNftLoanLiquidatePrice function in GenericLogic.sol explicitly sets liquidatePrice = max(nftPrice*(1-bonus), borrowAmount), meaning liquidatePrice >= borrowAmount always holds. When an NFT's market value drops significantly below the accumulated debt (which includes compounding interest), no rational bidder will place a bid because they'd pay more than the NFT is worth. The loan remains in ACTIVE status with health factor < 1, interest continues accruing (making the situation worse), and lender funds backing the loan become permanently inaccessible. Critically, there is no admin mechanism in the codebase to force-close underwater positions, write off bad debt, or allow sub-debt-value liquidation. The only admin function visible is collectFeeToTreasury, and emergency admin can only pause, not resolve stuck positions.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal is accurate. IsolateLogic.sol executeIsolateAuction enforces bid >= borrowAmount for first bids. GenericLogic.sol calculateNftLoanLiquidatePrice confirms liquidatePrice is floored at borrowAmount. No alternative liquidation path or admin intervention mechanism exists in the codebase.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The first bid in an isolate auction must be >= borrowAmount. When the NFT floor price is significantly below the accumulated debt, no rational liquidator will bid, making the position permanently unliquidatable.

liquidatePrice is explicitly floored at borrowAmount, meaning the minimum acceptable bid can never be less than the full debt amount. This guarantees that when debt >> NFT value, liquidation requires paying more than the NFT is worth.

calling_sequence: 2. InterestLogic.updateInterestIndexs updates borrow index, accruing more interest
4. liquidatePrice = max(nftPrice*(1-bonus), borrowAmount), so liquidatePrice >= borrowAmount
5. First bid requires: params.amounts[nidx] >= vars.borrowAmount AND >= vars.liquidatePrice
6. When borrowAmount >> nftPrice, bid must exceed NFT value, making liquidation economically irrational
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The path starts at isolateAuction entry point, flows through executeIsolateAuction which calls calculateNftLoanLiquidatePrice. The liquidatePrice calculation in GenericLogic explicitly floors the price at borrowAmount (line ~260: 'if (vars.liquidatePrice < vars.borrowAmount) { vars.liquidatePrice = vars.borrowAmount; }'). Back in executeIsolateAuction, the require statements enforce bid >= borrowAmount and bid >= liquidatePrice. Since liquidatePrice >= borrowAmount, the effective minimum bid is max(borrowAmount, liquidatePrice) = liquidatePrice = borrowAmount (when debt exceeds NFF value). No alternative path exists to liquidate underwater positions - there is no admin force-liquidation function, no bad debt write-off mechanism, and no governance-triggered market-price liquidation.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: InitConfigPool.s.sol shows NFT collateral parameters: liquidationThreshold of 8000 bp (80%), collateralFactor of 6000 bp (60%), liquidationBonus of 1000 bp (10%). These parameters mean borrowers can borrow up to 60% of NFT value, and liquidation triggers at 80%. A ~40% NFT price drop could create underwater positions where debt exceeds value.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Scenario: User deposits BAYC (floor 10 ETH) in isolate mode, borrows 5 ETH (at 60% LTV). Over time, BAYC floor drops to 3 ETH while debt grows to 6 ETH with interest. borrowAmount (6 ETH) > thresholdPrice (3 * 0.8 = 2.4 ETH), so auction is eligible. But the minimum bid must be >= 6 ETH for an NFT worth 3 ETH. No liquidator will take a guaranteed 3 ETH loss. The position remains in ACTIVE state with HF < 1, interest accrues further, and the bad debt grows. Lenders who supplied the 5 ETH (now 6 ETH with interest) cannot recover their funds from this loan. With configured auction params (auctionDuration = 1 day), there's no time-based fallback either.
validation_result.impact_assessment: The impact is systemic: during major NFT market downturns, multiple isolate loans could simultaneously become underwater. Each underwater loan permanently locks the borrowed ERC20 liquidity. If enough positions go underwater, the pool's availableLiquidity could be significantly reduced, potentially triggering a bank run scenario where remaining lenders race to withdraw before liquidity is exhausted. The protocol has no visible mechanism to socialize bad debt or force-close underwater positions. The deployment script shows LTV of 6000 bp, so a >40% NFT price decline (historically common in NFT markets) combined with accrued interest could trigger this scenario.
recommended_mitigations: Implement a Dutch auction mechanism where the minimum bid decreases over time, eventually allowing market-price liquidation and socializing bad debt across all lenders
Add an admin function to force-liquidate underwater positions at current market price, with bad debt absorbed by a protocol reserve fund or socialized across the pool
Implement a bad debt backstop mechanism (e.g., insurance fund, safety module) that absorbs losses when bid < borrowAmount
Allow bids below borrowAmount after a grace period (e.g., 7 days without any bid), accepting partial debt recovery over no recovery
Add governance-controlled parameter to cap the minimum bid at some multiple of NFT value rather than always requiring full debt coverage
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy protocol with test NFT and ERC20. 2. Deposit NFT in isolate mode, borrow at max LTV. 3. Use mock oracle to drop NFT price to 30% of original. 4. Wait for interest to accrue so borrowAmount > NFT value. 5. Attempt to call isolateAuction with bid = NFT value - verify revert with ISOLATE_BID_PRICE_LESS_THAN_BORROW. 6. Verify no other mechanism can close the position. 7. Confirm interest continues accruing on the stuck loan.
uncertainties: There may be an admin/governance mechanism in contracts not included in the analysis scope (e.g., Configurator module, PoolManager) that could handle bad debt
The protocol may intend to upgrade modules to add bad debt handling before deploying to mainnet (currently only on Sepolia)
External monitoring/keeper systems could potentially detect and liquidate positions before they become deeply underwater, though this doesn't eliminate the risk
additional_testing_needed: Test with historical NFT price data (BAYC, CryptoPunks) to quantify how often positions would become permanently stuck
Model the cascading effect of multiple underwater positions on pool liquidity and withdrawal availability
Verify all admin/governance functions in Configurator and other modules for any emergency bad debt handling capability
Test the interaction between yield managers and stuck isolate positions
